architecture.summary: LangFIR discovers sparse language-specific features from monolingual data by decomposing residual activations using sparse autoencoders (SAEs), then filters out language-agnostic features using random-token sequences to isolate language-identity directions. These sparse feature directions are aggregated into steering vectors that are added to model activations at inference time to control multilingual output language.
trained_components: sparse autoencoder (SAE) over residual stream activations

biblio.title: LangFIR: Discovering Sparse Language-Specific Features from Monolingual Data for Language Steering

claims.statement: LangFIR discovers language-specific SAE features using only a small amount of monolingual data and random-token sequences, without requiring multilingual or parallel data.

Many SAE features consistently activated by target-language inputs do not encode language identity; random-token sequences surface these language-agnostic features, allowing filtering to isolate language-specific features.

Language-specific features discovered by LangFIR are extremely sparse, highly selective for their target language, and causally important: directional ablation increases cross-entropy loss only for the corresponding language.

LangFIR achieves the best average accuracy BLEU across three models (Gemma 3 1B, Gemma 3 4B, and Llama 3.1 8B), three datasets, and twelve target languages, outperforming the strongest monolingual baseline.
LangFIR surpasses methods that rely on parallel data for multilingual generation control.
Language identity in multilingual LLMs is localized in a sparse set of feature directions discoverable with monolingual data.
